note: Seed backfill compiled from major public AVSEC events Jul 2025 - May 2026. Best-effort major-event reconstruction, thinner than the live daily pull. The daily Action accumulates fresh data from here.

days.briefing: Scattered Spider's social-engineering campaign sweeps global aviation, hitting carriers across three continents; analysts warn cyberattacks on the sector have surged sharply year-on-year.

items.title: Qantas breach exposes up to 6M customer records
items.summary: Scattered Spider suspected in a breach exposing personal data of up to six million Qantas customers via a third party.

items.title: Scattered Spider hits WestJet and Hawaiian Airlines
items.summary: Coordinated social-engineering attacks disrupted internal systems and apps at North American carriers.

items.title: Air France-KLM third-party platform breached
items.summary: Unauthorised activity detected on an external customer-service platform used by the carriers.

items.title: Thales: aviation cyberattacks up ~600%
items.summary: Thales reported aviation cyberattacks rose roughly 600 percent year-on-year, with dozens of ransomware incidents logged.

days.briefing: A quieter month for incidents but heavy on rule-making: regulators on both sides of the Atlantic tighten aviation cyber obligations.

items.title: TSA issues aviation cybersecurity amendments
items.summary: TSA rolled out amendments mandating stronger cyber controls and incident-reporting protocols for the aviation sector.

items.title: EASA Part-IS information-security rules near deadline
items.summary: EASA Part-IS requirements extend information-security duties to airlines, airports and ANSPs with phased deadlines into 2026.

items.title: ICAO advances Cybersecurity Action Plan
items.summary: ICAO pushed its Cybersecurity Action Plan focusing on governance, faster incident response and security-by-design.

days.briefing: Europe's worst aviation security week in years: a supply-chain ransomware attack paralyses major-hub check-in while a wave of airport drone incursions triggers hybrid-threat fears.

items.title: Collins Aerospace MUSE ransomware grounds check-in
items.summary: HardBit ransomware crippled the Collins MUSE check-in platform, forcing manual processing at Heathrow, Brussels, Berlin and Dublin.

items.title: Copenhagen and Oslo airports shut by drones
items.summary: Large drones forced a four-hour Copenhagen shutdown and a brief Oslo closure; Denmark called it a hybrid attack on critical infrastructure.

items.title: Wave of drone closures across Danish airports
items.summary: Repeated sightings closed Aalborg, Billund and other Danish airports over several nights, with military sites also affected.

items.title: ENISA confirms airport outage was ransomware
items.summary: The EU cyber agency ENISA confirmed the Collins airport disruption as a ransomware attack and opened investigations.

days.briefing: The drone disruption spreads south and west across Europe; governments increasingly frame the incursions as state-linked hybrid activity.

items.title: Munich Airport suspends flights twice over drones
items.summary: Munich halted operations twice within 24 hours over drone sightings, stranding thousands of passengers.

items.title: Brussels and Liege airports closed by drones
items.summary: Belgian airports briefly closed amid sightings; drones also seen over military bases and a nuclear plant.

items.title: Germany assesses drones as likely Russian espionage
items.summary: German officials judged many overflights as probable state-linked espionage aimed at unsettling the public.

days.briefing: Counter-drone response moves from improvisation to coordination, with allied deployments in Europe and a US legal-authority push.

items.title: Allies send counter-drone help to Belgium
items.summary: Near-daily incursions over Belgian airports and bases prompted UK, France and Germany to deploy counter-drone support.

items.title: US drone incursions near airports up 25%+
items.summary: FAA data showed a sharp rise in airport drone incursions, intensifying the counter-UAS authority debate in Congress.

days.briefing: Year-end sees authorities hard-coding the autumn drone lessons into temporary bans and broader take-down powers.

items.title: Denmark and EU impose drone bans around summits
items.summary: Temporary civilian drone bans were placed around airports and critical sites during high-profile EU events.

items.title: US expands counter-UAS take-down authority
items.summary: Congress moved to let more federal, state and local agencies disable rogue drones, subject to training.

days.briefing: The new year opens with drones firmly reframed from nuisance to national-security and business-continuity risk.

items.title: Cartel border drones reframed as security threat
items.summary: Washington's terrorist designation of cartels recast persistent border drone activity as a national-security matter.

items.title: 2026 outlook: drones as a repeatable disruption tactic
items.summary: Analysts warned drone incursions now behave like a low-cost, repeatable business-interruption tactic against critical sites.

days.briefing: Interagency miscoordination over counter-drone lasers shuts a major US airport twice in a month, the most disruptive domestic airspace closures since 9/11.

items.title: FAA abruptly shuts El Paso airspace, then reverses
items.summary: The FAA grounded El Paso flights for a stated 10 days then lifted it hours later, tied to a counter-drone laser test near Fort Bliss.

items.title: Second laser incident closes El Paso airspace again
items.summary: Two weeks later the military downed a CBP drone with a laser, prompting another El Paso closure and congressional criticism.

days.briefing: A quieter month dominated by follow-through: regulators press supply-chain resilience after the autumn ransomware shock.

items.title: Post-Collins aviation supply-chain resilience review
items.summary: Reviews pushed EU supply-chain resilience and NIS2-aligned incident-reporting expectations for aviation technology vendors.

days.briefing: A fatal perimeter breach at a major US hub puts airfield intrusion and perimeter integrity back in the spotlight.

items.title: Denver airport perimeter breach fatality
items.summary: A man breached Denver International's perimeter and was killed on a runway as a jet took off, reviving scrutiny of perimeter security.

days.briefing: Global aviation security remains under significant pressure in mid-2026, with GPS spoofing attacks targeting Indian airports, a fatal perimeter breach at Denver International, and persistent cyber threats against aviation supply chains dominating the threat landscape. Regulatory activity is intensifying, with the FAA advancing long-delayed counter-UAS rulemaking, EASA's Part-IS cybersecurity mandate now fully in force, and ICAO's 2026-2028 Global Aviation Security Plan directing new work on weaponised UAS and GNSS interference.

items.title: GPS Spoofing Cyberattack Hits Seven Major Indian Airports
items.summary: India confirmed GPS spoofing at seven airports including Delhi, Mumbai and Bengaluru, delivering false navigation data during landing; no flights cancelled.

items.title: Fatal Perimeter Breach at Denver International Airport Triggers Security Review
items.summary: A man breached Denver Airport's perimeter on 8 May and was killed on an active runway, exposing critical perimeter security gaps.

items.title: FAA Publishes Long-Awaited NPRM for Drone Flight Restrictions Over Critical Infrastructure
items.summary: FAA's Section 2209 NPRM, published 6 May 2026, establishes drone flight restriction zones over critical facilities; comments due 6 July 2026.

items.title: Aviation Booking Software Provider Breached in MFA-Fatigue Phishing Campaign
items.summary: March 2026 phishing attack compromised an airline booking software provider's IT admin credentials via MFA fatigue, exposing cloud and OT systems.

items.title: ICAO 2026–2028 Global Aviation Security Plan Targets Weaponised UAS and GNSS Threats
items.summary: ICAO Assembly adopted the Second Edition Global Aviation Security Plan, directing new work programme on weaponised UAS and GNSS radio frequency interference.

items.title: EASA Part-IS Information Security Mandate Now Fully Binding Across EU Aviation
items.summary: EASA Part-IS cybersecurity regulation became fully applicable as of February 2026, covering airlines, airports, MROs and ATC with mandatory ISMS requirements.

days.briefing: Global aviation security faces a high-tempo threat environment driven by escalating GPS/GNSS spoofing campaigns targeting multiple countries, a sustained wave of cyber intrusions against airline and airport IT infrastructure, and fresh physical disruptions to critical airport facilities. Regulatory bodies including EASA, FAA, and ICAO are actively issuing conflict-zone bulletins, UAS restriction frameworks, and mandatory cybersecurity compliance deadlines in response to these converging threats.

items.title: Caracas Simón Bolívar Airport Shut Down After Earthquake Destroys Terminal
items.summary: Twin earthquakes caused severe structural damage to Venezuela's main international gateway, suspending all flights pending full safety inspections of screening areas and infrastructure.

items.title: EASA Issues New Conflict Zone Bulletin on Iran, Iraq and Lebanon Airspace
items.summary: EASA CZIB 2026-03-R12 directs operators to avoid Iran, Iraq and Lebanon airspace and exercise caution across Bahrain, Israel, Jordan, Kuwait, Qatar, UAE, Oman and Saudi Arabia.

items.title: FAA Publishes Long-Awaited NPRM to Restrict Drone Flights Over Critical Infrastructure
items.summary: FAA's Section 2209 NPRM establishes a process for facility owners to apply for UAS flight restrictions; public comment period closes 6 July 2026.

items.title: Aviation Booking Software Provider Hit by MFA-Fatigue Phishing Attack Affecting Multiple Airlines
items.summary: Threat actors used social engineering and MFA fatigue to compromise an IT administrator's credentials, gaining access to identity systems, Microsoft 365 and OT environments.

items.title: EASA Part-IS Information Security Implementing Regulation Now Fully Applicable
items.summary: EASA's Part-IS cybersecurity rule became fully applicable on 22 February 2026, mandating Information Security Management Systems for airlines, airports, MROs and air navigation service providers.

items.title: India Confirms Coordinated GPS Spoofing Campaign Targeting Eight Major Airports
items.summary: India's Civil Aviation Minister confirmed GPS spoofing and GNSS jamming at Delhi, Mumbai, Kolkata, Amritsar, Hyderabad, Bengaluru and Chennai airports; source unattributed, state-sponsored actor suspected.

days.briefing: The global aviation security environment over the past 72 hours has been marked by a deadly JNIM terrorist assault on Niamey's international airport in Niger and confirmed GPS spoofing attacks across seven major Indian airports, underscoring persistent physical and cyber threats to civil aviation worldwide. Simultaneously, the EU Council circulated a new Aviation Security Strategy document on 19 June 2026, and the FAA's critical infrastructure drone-restriction rulemaking (NPRM) entered its public-comment phase, reflecting accelerating regulatory responses to evolving UAS and hybrid threats.

items.title: JNIM Militants Storm Niamey International Airport; 13 Killed
items.summary: Al-Qaeda-linked JNIM attacked Diori Hamani Airport on 18 Jun 2026, killing 13; 22 attackers killed, 20 arrested. Second airport attack in 2026.

items.title: GPS Spoofing Cyberattack Hits Seven Major Indian Airports
items.summary: India confirmed GPS spoofing at Delhi, Mumbai, Kolkata, Hyderabad and Bengaluru airports, feeding false navigation data to approaching aircraft; no flights cancelled.

items.title: EU Council Circulates New Aviation Security Strategy Document
items.summary: Council document COM(2026)229 dated 19 Jun proposes faster classified-information sharing, cargo supply-chain hardening, and a conflict-zone risk-assessment mechanism with EASA/EEAS.

items.title: FAA Publishes NPRM for Drone Flight Restrictions Over Critical Infrastructure
items.summary: FAA published long-awaited Section 2209 NPRM on 6 May 2026 to allow facility operators to apply for UAS flight restrictions; public comments due 6 July 2026.

items.title: FAA Reports 100+ Monthly Drone Incursions Near Airports Amid World Cup Security Pressure
items.summary: FAA receives over 100 monthly drone sighting reports near airports; experts warn counter-UAS readiness remains uneven across federal, state and local agencies ahead of FIFA World Cup.

items.title: Korean Air Data Breach Exposes 30,000 Employee Records via Oracle EBS Campaign
items.summary: Hackers exploited Oracle EBS zero-days to steal Korean Air employee names and bank details from catering subsidiary KC&D; Envoy Air also confirmed victim of same campaign.

days.briefing: Global aviation security is currently grappling with a dual challenge: immediate operational threats such as drone incursions near critical infrastructure, and evolving regulatory landscapes impacted by new border control systems and advanced cyber risks. Geopolitical tensions continue to necessitate heightened vigilance and coordinated policy responses across major regions to safeguard international air travel.

items.title: United Airlines Pilot Reports Near-Collision with Drone at Newark Airport
items.summary: A United Airlines Boeing 737 pilot reported a near-miss with a three-foot-wide circular drone during descent to Newark Liberty International Airport, raising significant safety concerns.
items.fullContent: On Friday, June 26, 2026, a United Airlines Boeing 737, carrying 106 passengers and five crew members from Key West, Florida, experienced a near-collision with an unidentified drone while approaching Newark Liberty International Airport. The pilot reported to air traffic control that the circular, approximately three-foot-wide drone was observed "about 100 feet below us" during the aircraft's descent at approximately 5:30 p.m. local time. The aircraft landed safely without further incident, and passengers disembarked as normal. This incident was corroborated by another pilot operating a United Express flight by GoJet Airlines, who also reported a drone sighting at roughly 2,000 feet near Newark around the same time. The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) strictly prohibits drone operations in controlled airspaces or near manned aircraft without official authorization, highlighting the illegality and inherent danger of such incursions. The FAA records approximately 100 drone sightings near U.S. airports monthly, emphasizing a persistent challenge to aviation safety. Authorities, including the FAA, GoJet, and Newark airport, have been contacted for further information as investigations into this specific near-miss are underway. The increasing frequency of unauthorized drone activity near airports poses a significant threat of catastrophic mid-air collisions, underscoring the urgent need for enhanced drone detection and mitigation technologies and stricter enforcement of airspace regulations.

items.title: Sri Lanka Assumes Chairmanship of Asia-Pacific Aviation Security Steering Committee
items.summary: Sri Lanka hosted and assumed the chairmanship of the Cooperative Aviation Security Program–Asia Pacific (CASP-AP) Steering Committee, positioning it at the forefront of regional aviation security policy.
items.fullContent: Sri Lanka has taken on a significant leadership role in regional aviation security by hosting and assuming the chairmanship of the Cooperative Aviation Security Program–Asia Pacific (CASP-AP) Steering Committee for the 2026-2027 term. This development follows the Cabinet of Ministers' approval last week to host two major Asia-Pacific aviation security summits. The 21st Steering Committee Meeting of CASP-AP took place on June 23-24, and the 14th Regional Aviation Security Coordination Forum (RASCF) for Asia and the Pacific was held on June 25-26, 2026. These meetings attracted approximately 80 delegates, including government representatives, international organizations, and aviation security experts from 41 countries across the Asia-Pacific region. Assuming the chairmanship places Sri Lanka at the center of regional aviation security policymaking, a role viewed by the government as an opportunity to strengthen cooperation with regional partners and enhance its profile as a strategic aviation hub in the Indian Ocean. The summits facilitated crucial discussions on future security frameworks, intelligence-sharing mechanisms, and regional preparedness measures, all aimed at safeguarding the rapidly expanding aviation sector in the Asia-Pacific. CASP-AP functions as the principal decision-making body for aviation security programs in the region, making Sri Lanka's new leadership position influential in shaping security standards and collaborative efforts.

items.title: EASA Extends Middle East Airspace Conflict Zone Warning Amid Lingering Threats
items.summary: EASA prolonged its Middle East flight warning until July 1, 2026, advising European airlines to avoid Iranian, Iraqi, and Lebanese airspace due to ongoing security risks.
items.fullContent: On June 24, 2026, the European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) officially extended its Conflict Zone Information Bulletin (CZIB) for the Middle East, advising European commercial airlines to maintain strict flight restrictions through at least July 1, 2026. The advisory specifically warns against operating in the airspace over Iran, Iraq, and Lebanon at all flight levels and altitudes, citing persistent volatile security threats and a critical risk of aircraft misidentification. Additionally, operators are urged to exercise caution when flying within the airspace of Bahrain, Kuwait, Israel, Jordan, Qatar, Oman, Saudi Arabia, and the United Arab Emirates. This decision, emanating from EASA headquarters in Cologne, Germany, follows a comprehensive risk assessment conducted in coordination with EU member states. Despite a fragile diplomatic framework and a temporary ceasefire agreement between Washington and Tehran, which initially took effect on April 8, 2026, regulators remain highly skeptical of long-term regional stability. The CZIB, initially issued on February 28, 2026, after military strikes between the United States and Israel against Iranian targets, has been extended multiple times as the security situation remains tense. EASA highlighted the ongoing possibility of short-term breaches of the ceasefire, particularly in and around the Strait of Hormuz. The agency emphasizes that while the overall risk level has decreased, the sustainability of the truce is uncertain, with a potential for rapid escalation. Airlines are mandated to continuously audit their regional route networks, implement strict navigation monitoring, and develop contingency protocols for rerouting flights to mitigate hazards.

items.title: European Airports Warn of 'Chaos' Over New EU Entry/Exit System Biometric Checks
items.summary: Rome airports and IATA are warning of potential 6-hour queues and operational chaos during summer due to technical glitches with the EU's new biometric Entry/Exit System (EES).
items.fullContent: Major European airports, including those in Rome (Fiumicino and Ciampino), have issued urgent warnings regarding the potential for "operational chaos" and prolonged passenger queues due to the new European Union Entry/Exit System (EES). The EES, which became fully operational in mid-April 2026 after phased rollout from October 2025, requires non-EU citizens to provide fingerprints and facial scans upon their first entry into the Schengen Area, replacing traditional passport stamps. Marco Troncone, CEO of Aeroporti di Roma, rated the risk level for severe bottlenecks during the peak summer tourist months at "eight or nine" out of ten, arguing that the new biometric registration process is incompatible with anticipated traffic volumes. He stressed the necessity for a temporary suspension of certain biometric checks to prevent severe congestion. Since its launch, the system has been plagued by chronic technical glitches, including malfunctions at self-service kiosks and errors forcing already registered passengers to repeat the entire process. Olivier Jankovec, Director General of ACI Europe, criticized the automated systems for not functioning as intended. The International Air Transport Association (IATA) has echoed these concerns, warning that wait times at the most affected airports could reach up to six hours. Some airports, like those in Greece, have already begun applying exception criteria, temporarily allowing British citizens to bypass EES procedures to alleviate congestion. The issue is not confined to the EU, with hubs like London and Istanbul expressing worries about a potential domino effect of flight delays across the global aviation network.

items.title: ACI Europe Highlights Growing Threat of AI-Enabled Cyberattacks in Aviation
items.summary: ACI Europe Director General warned that AI-enabled cyberattacks pose an escalating, systemic threat to aviation, calling for reduced complexity and shared intelligence to enhance resilience.
items.fullContent: On June 25, 2026, Olivier Jankovec, Director General of ACI Europe, issued a stark warning about the escalating threat of AI-enabled cyberattacks targeting the aviation industry. He emphasized that this challenge can no longer be deferred and requires a fundamental shift in how airports approach cybersecurity, moving beyond individual defenses to address systemic vulnerabilities. OneReg, an aviation compliance and safety software platform, supports this view, identifying two key priorities for the industry: reducing complexity and leveraging collective intelligence. Jankovec highlighted that many airport technology environments have evolved organically over decades, resulting in fragmented landscapes with legacy systems, standalone solutions, and bespoke integrations. This creates a larger attack surface with numerous vendors and integration points where vulnerabilities can emerge. As AI-enabled threats become capable of rapidly identifying and exploiting these weaknesses, consolidating operational processes onto modern, unified platforms with robust security controls and continuous patching has become a security imperative. Furthermore, Jankovec stressed the importance of collective intelligence, noting that cybersecurity threats are rarely unique to a single organization. He argued that the industry could learn and adapt faster than attackers by sharing knowledge and collaborative learning within ecosystems that combine resilient platforms with shared intelligence. This comprehensive approach is deemed essential to enhance overall aviation security against increasingly sophisticated and rapidly evolving cyber threats.

items.title: Federal Grand Jury Indicts 12 in Major Prison Drone Smuggling Operation
items.summary: A federal grand jury indicted 12 individuals for operating a drone smuggling ring that delivered drugs, phones, and weapons into 10 federal prisons across eight U.S. states.
items.fullContent: On June 24, 2026, a federal grand jury in the Middle District of Georgia unsealed a 17-count indictment against twelve individuals involved in what prosecutors describe as the largest federal case of coordinated drone smuggling into prisons. The conspiracy, which ran from September 2023 until May 2026, involved using drones to deliver contraband such as methamphetamine, marijuana, other controlled substances, cell phones, tobacco, and even saw blades for potential escapes, into ten federal prisons across eight U.S. states. The targeted facilities included Federal Correctional Institute Atlanta and Jesup in Georgia, Federal Medical Center Lexington and Federal Correctional Institute Manchester in Kentucky, Federal Correctional Institute Memphis in Tennessee, Federal Correctional Complex Petersburg in Virginia, Federal Correctional Institute Pollock in Louisiana, Federal Correctional Institute Talladega in Alabama, and Federal Correctional Institute Yazoo City in Mississippi. Among those indicted are Ira Christopher Jackson, also known as “Chris,” “CJ,” “Action,” “Action Jackson,” 42, of Macon; Kenna Middleton, 45; Leviticus Blash, 42; Chrystal Dunn, 37; and brothers Jeff and Tysean Richardson, both 23. Prosecutors allege that Middleton assisted in drone operations and inmate communication, Blash aided with drop operations, and Dunn served as a driver and lookout. The Richardson brothers were accused of flying drones for the deliveries. The U.S. Attorney, William R. Keyes, emphasized that this bust demonstrates a robust response to high-tech methods of introducing contraband, utilizing sophisticated drone detection systems that provide alerts, aircraft identification, and track flight paths and launch locations. Some defendants also face charges for owning and operating unregistered drones, highlighting the application of federal aviation law in prosecuting such criminal activities.

items.title: Airbus and Kawasaki Heavy Industries Partner on Japanese Maritime Eurodrone Variant
items.summary: Airbus signed an MoU with Kawasaki Heavy Industries to explore a Japanese anti-submarine warfare variant of the U950 Eurodrone, enhancing maritime security capabilities.
items.fullContent: On June 26, 2026, Airbus announced the signing of a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with Kawasaki Heavy Industries to collaborate on exploring a Japanese anti-submarine warfare (ASW) variant of the U950 Eurodrone. The Eurodrone, recognized as Europe's first Large Long Endurance Remotely Piloted Aircraft System (RPAS), is being developed through a four-nation program involving Germany, France, Italy, and Spain, led by the Organisation for Joint Armament Cooperation (OCCAR). Japan has held observer status in the Eurodrone program since 2023, indicating its strategic interest in the platform. This collaboration aims to analyze opportunities for a specific Japanese ASW variant, which would complement Japan's existing crewed ASW fleet with a highly efficient uncrewed platform, thereby strengthening its maritime security in a sovereign and sustainable manner. The Eurodrone is well-suited for countries requiring extensive maritime surveillance due to its long flight endurance and substantial mission payload capacity, which can include sonobuoys and torpedoes for ASW. The next steps involve discussions to define possible configurations, integrate Japanese sensors and effectors, and determine potential workshare for Japanese industry in production and sustainment phases. The goal is to ensure Japan can operate the Eurodrone autonomously if it decides to acquire the RPAS. This partnership not only bolsters the Eurodrone program but also deepens strategic defense initiatives between Europe and Japan, with insights from the Japanese variant expected to benefit future European naval versions.
